message.content: Adversaries may exploit hidden file systems to conceal malicious activities from users and security tools. These hidden file systems, also known as virtual file systems, are separate from standard file systems like FAT, NTFS, ext4, and APFS. By using hidden file systems, adversaries can obscure the presence of malicious components and file operations. Implementations vary, including storing file systems in reserved disk space, dropping portable partition images, or fragmenting files in non-standard ways. Notable examples include BOOTRASH using unallocated disk space for the Nemesis bootkit, ComRAT placing a FAT16 partition image in %TEMP%, Equation storing an encrypted virtual file system in the Windows Registry, and Regin and Strider using hidden file systems for component storage. The impact of such techniques includes evasion of detection and prolonged persistence on compromised systems. Mitigation strategies involve regular monitoring of disk space usage, integrity checks of file systems, and employing advanced forensic tools to detect anomalies in file system structures.
